subjective.chief_complaint: She's had a fever today and she's just not herself.
history_of_present_illness: This is Aria, she's two months old.
Around mid-morning she felt a bit warm, but we thought maybe it was just the weather.
But in the afternoon she got really hot, and we checked her temperature - it was 38.7.
Ear thermometer, we did it twice.
She's usually quite alert, even at this age.
But today she keeps dozing off during feeds.
Today she's had about 50 or 60mls at best.
Normally she has four or five by now.
Her dad has a bit of a cold, nothing serious.
Yes, she had them right on time.
She just seems so floppy compared to usual.
associated_symptoms: She's had a fever today and she's just not herself.
Just one little vomit earlier.

exam_findings: I'm just going to pop this thermometer under her arm and check her temp, and I'll have a look at her colour and breathing while I do that.
(pauses, checks temp, listens, palpates, watches breathing)
Her temperature is still up - 38.5 - and I can see her heart rate is a bit fast, which is normal with fever.
Her breathing looks stable, nice and even, and her colour is okay.
I'm going to check capillary refill on her chest… (presses) That's good - comes back quickly.

assessment: Febrile infant <3 months requiring urgent review
She needs to be seen pretty urgently, and we'll likely do a septic workup - bloods, urine, maybe even other tests depending on what the doctor thinks.
plan: I'm going to check capillary refill on her chest… (presses) That's good - comes back quickly.

For now, we'll get you straight through rather than waiting in the waiting room.
I'm going to pop an ID band on her wrist - this won't hurt - and I'm going to put you as "priority seen urgently." We'll take her straight into the paediatric assessment area.

Maybe a tiny bit but not much.
He's too puffed to drink properly.
negatives: Not blue, but he's been really distressed and breathing so hard.
relevant_history: Yes, he was diagnosed last year.

exam_findings: It's going to check your oxygen.
(pauses) Oxygen is 92%.
He's breathing fast and using his tummy muscles.
observed_general_state: He's definitely working harder than we'd like.

He needs urgent asthma treatment - nebulisers, maybe steroids.
plan: Come on through, let's get your little one comfortable.
He needs urgent asthma treatment - nebulisers, maybe steroids.
Hard to say yet - some kids improve quickly with hospital treatment, others need monitoring for a day or two.
I'm going to put an ID band on him and take you straight through to acute care.
